anchor_facts.fact: Nearly nine out of ten syntax errors concentrate in a single CID category (lint/QoR improvement tasks), caused by the model introducing inline variable declarations inside procedural blocks that the open-source simulator rejects, revealing a systematic toolchain-awareness failure.

In flaky dot-product tasks, the difference between passing and failing rollouts is whether the model correctly separates sequential state updates (registered outputs with latency counters) from combinational next-state logic — passing rollouts use always_ff for output timing while failing rollouts conflate sequential and combinational assignments.

Roughly 4% of simulation mismatches are AttributeError failures where the testbench cannot find expected signals in the model's RTL, indicating port/signal naming mismatches rather than logic errors — the model uses different names than the testbench expects.
Most syntax failures trace back to one repeated pattern: introducing procedural-scope declarations and other advanced SystemVerilog constructs that the benchmark toolchain does not accept.
Syntax errors are highly concentrated in the Lint/QoR Improvement category.

Parse failures are extremely rare (well under 1% of rollouts) and concentrate in just two tasks from the same topic (elevator control), suggesting a narrow, topic-specific failure to produce extractable Verilog rather than a systemic output formatting issue.

Across every category and difficulty slice, failing attempts are longer and use substantially more generation budget than passing attempts, so errors correlate with overextended incorrect implementations rather than under-generation.

Syntax failures are highly concentrated in the optimization/lint-oriented category, revealing that code-modification tasks trigger a distinct toolchain-compatibility failure mode not seen broadly elsewhere.

That optimization/lint category uniquely suffers from a dual-validator trap: the model often introduces code that is rejected either by the simulator parser or by lint checks, so attempted 'improvements' frequently worsen compliance.
All lint-check failures occur exclusively in the Lint/QoR Improvement category, meaning the model often introduces new lint violations when attempting to fix existing ones.
The code completion category (cid002) has the highest always-fail rate of any CID (roughly 60%), making it the weakest task type despite being the most straightforward code generation task.

Timeout failures concentrate in a small number of tasks (roughly a dozen), with two tasks timing out on all five rollouts and several others timing out on three or more, suggesting these represent specific design patterns (like non-terminating FSMs) rather than random failures.

The weakest category is code completion rather than design-from-spec or bug-fixing, suggesting the model struggles more when it must infer and preserve partially given structure than when it can synthesize a fresh design plan.

Errors indicating unknown or high-impedance signal propagation are significantly more prevalent in flaky tasks than in always-fail tasks, suggesting non-deterministic signal initialization is a key driver of inconsistency.

In a representative sorting task, the model implements the correct sorting algorithm but reverses the bit-packing order when mapping the internal array to the flattened output bus — a correct-algorithm, wrong-interface failure pattern where the computation is right but the output formatting is wrong.

A recurring subset of simulation failures are actually interface mismatches surfaced as missing-signal access errors, showing that some 'logic' failures come from naming or hierarchy divergence from the expected DUT contract.
The root cause of most syntax errors is the model introducing SystemVerilog constructs, like inline variable declarations inside procedural blocks, that the specific open-source toolchain rejects.

Median execution time is slightly lower for failing rollouts than passing ones, because many logic failures are caught quickly by the first test assertion, despite a few long timeouts skewing the mean.

The consistency gap between the oracle ceiling (best-of-five) and actual pass@1 is roughly 19 percentage points — nearly as large as the base pass rate itself — indicating that almost as much performance is lost to inconsistency as to missing capability.

All output was produced through the normal code output channel — zero rollouts fell back to reasoning-verbatim or reasoning-parsed paths, meaning the model reliably generates text through its primary output channel even when the content is wrong.

Control & FSM tasks have the lowest pass rate of any domain (around 13%), with timeout failures also concentrating in this domain, suggesting the model generates FSMs with incorrect termination conditions that produce non-halting simulations.

ValueError failures (X/Z propagation) are roughly 2.5 times more prevalent in flaky task failures than in always-fail task failures, suggesting that non-deterministic signal initialization is a key driver of inconsistency — the model sometimes gets reset logic right and sometimes doesn't.

Bug fixing tasks (cid016) lead all CID categories in pass rate (approaching 38%), suggesting the model performs better when given buggy code to fix than when generating or completing code from scratch.

On a protocol task (APB GPIO controller), the model correctly implements the bus interface, register map, and basic I/O (passing roughly 4 out of 10 sub-tests) but fails on interrupt generation logic requiring multi-register, multi-condition reasoning about edge/level detection and read-to-clear behavior.
About 3% of simulation mismatches involve ValueError from X/Z propagation to outputs, typically caused by incomplete reset logic leaving registers uninitialized or undriven signals in the design.
